coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of carbon quantum dots by microemulsion route: Dual functional materials for energy storage and environmental treatment

coredata.dc:description: In this research, Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized by the microemulsion method with a variation of 1- 5 wt. % multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs). Varying percentages of MWCNTs affected morphological, structural, antibacterial, photocatalytic, dielectric, electrochemical and optical properties of CQDs. X-ray diffractometer confirmed the formation of the cubic phase of carbon. SEM revealed a granular and a cloud like surface morphology that was not uniform, with a significant number of grain boundaries. FTIR spectroscopy was used to examine the different functional groups. As MWCNTs concentration increased, the band gap of CQDs reduced from 3.89 to 3.5 eV. These CQDs were suitable as absorbing layers for solar panels due to their reduced band gap and ability to absorb the visible portion of the electromagnetic spectrum. The dielectric properties of all CQDs were also examined. AC conductivity was increased with the increased MWCNTs content. Utilizing these CQDs considerably improved the efficiency of supercapacitors, resulting in specific capacitance ranging from 1007.81 to 4531.25 Fg-1 with high energy and power density. This led to the manufacturing of supercapacitors in modern electrical devices. Furthermore, increasing MWCNTs concentration enhanced the photocatalytic properties of CQDs. These synthesized nanoparticles exhibit improved photocatalytic activity due to increased UV–VIS light absorption and decreased recombination of photoinduced electron-hole pairs. CQDs have strong antibacterial activity, especially against salmonella enterica. These CQDs have significance in supercapacitors and water treatment.

head.ce:title: Fabrication of carbon quantum dots by microemulsion route: Dual functional materials for energy storage and environmental treatment

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Lahore College for Women University, Lahore, 54000, Pakistan
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Lahore College for Women University, Lahore, 54000, Pakistan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this research, Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized by the microemulsion method with a variation of 1- 5 wt. % multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs). Varying percentages of MWCNTs affected morphological, structural, antibacterial, photocatalytic, dielectric, electrochemical and optical properties of CQDs. X-ray diffractometer confirmed the formation of the cubic phase of carbon. SEM revealed a granular and a cloud like surface morphology that was not uniform, with a significant number of grain boundaries. FTIR spectroscopy was used to examine the different functional groups. As MWCNTs concentration increased, the band gap of CQDs reduced from 3.89 to 3.5 eV. These CQDs were suitable as absorbing layers for solar panels due to their reduced band gap and ability to absorb the visible portion of the electromagnetic spectrum. The dielectric properties of all CQDs were also examined. AC conductivity was increased with the increased MWCNTs content. Utilizing these CQDs considerably improved the efficiency of supercapacitors, resulting in specific capacitance ranging from 1007.81 to 4531.25 Fg-1 with high energy and power density. This led to the manufacturing of supercapacitors in modern electrical devices. Furthermore, increasing MWCNTs concentration enhanced the photocatalytic properties of CQDs. These synthesized nanoparticles exhibit improved photocatalytic activity due to increased UV–VIS light absorption and decreased recombination of photoinduced electron-hole pairs. CQDs have strong antibacterial activity, especially against salmonella enterica. These CQDs have significance in supercapacitors and water treatment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: In the nanotechnology era, a functional system can be engineered at the molecular level. Research on nanotechnology and the production of new nanomaterials significantly influence all branches of science, engineering and medicine. Quantum dots (QDs) are one of the main classes of nanoparticles widely used for a variety of industrial and biological applications. Since their inception, quantum dots (QDs) have been at the heart of nanoscience and nanotechnology and they continue to play an important role in the field's evolution [1].

Water and energy shortages are the two main problems modern humanity is confronting. Energy is needed to provide fresh water, while water is used to produce energy. Both of these routes confront increasing challenges and restrictions in the current environment. The depletion of fossil fuels has led to an energy crisis since it pollutes water sources, rendering them unfit for human consumption. In this study, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), also known as semiconductor nanocrystals with luminous capabilities, are recently created nanomaterials whose unique photo-physical features are creating a new generation of durable materials and sensors for sustainable development.

Quantum dots are nanoscale semiconductors with optical and other physical properties different from their bulk counterpart. A few nanometers in size, they are composed of inorganic substances either transition metals or rare earth metals made of a core substance encased in a shell of another semiconductor material. The size of QDs influences the characteristics including photoluminescence, absorbance and optical properties, in a dependent manner. The choice of quantum dots is based on the different domains in which they are used. The heavy or inorganic components that comprise the semiconductor outer shell of QDs, such as zinc oxide (ZnO), cadmium (Cd), silica, selenium (Se) and so on, are coated with shell material which provides a particular location for coupling and reduces their toxic effects [2].

Inorganic quantum dots are zinc oxide, graphene and carbon quantum dots (CQDs). Among them, the recently developed fluorescent CQDs a new member of the carbonaceous nanomaterial family, have attracted major interest worldwide in the field of nanoscience and nanotechnology due to their special benefits, which include simple formation techniques, good mechanical properties, affordability, photostability, outstanding water solubility, high optical absorptivity, low toxicity and tunable photoluminescence, which make it a suitable candidate for various applications [3]. Due to their unique and fascinating multipurpose properties, outstanding biocompatibility, and sustainable development, CQDs have provided an appealing platform for ecological, biological and energy-associated applications such as biological imaging, biosensors, medical care assessment, catalytic processes, multiple-color LED fabrication, medication, transmitting genes, phototherapy, food safety and conversion of energy [4–7].

A literature survey showed CQDs are generally derived from non-renewable resources, such as sugars [8], petroleum coke [9], coal [10], ethylamine [11], octadecenoic acid and formic acetic acid [12,13]. CQDs were also manufactured from organic materials such as waste products, food spoilage, and solid waste from municipalities because of their high quantity of carbon (45–55 w.t %) [14,15]. However, the process of refining them from raw biomass is complicated.

To observe the research gap, CQDs were prepared by using inorganic materials multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) and varying percentages of MWCNTs (1–5 w.t %). MWCNTs have remarkable active surface area, excellent durability and chemical inertness, making them optimal for multiple applications such as nano-sized metal-based or semi-conducting sections in energy storage devices [16–19].

Water contaminants as organic pollutants such as urea, pigments and organic solvents are hazardous to human health and the health of other living things. These organic dyes are primarily used in a variety of industrial sectors, including leather, cotton, paper, and other related industries. A significant amount of wastewater from industrial dyes is discharged and eventually enters the aquatic environment. These dyes' properties include persistence and non-biodegradability. To remove color from wastewater, a variety of materials have been employed, including graphene oxide, chitosan, activated carbon and biosorbents and silica. Due to their remarkable qualities, carbon-based materials have drawn a lot of attention [20]. CQDs have recently acquired popularity as a way to improve photocatalytic activity due to the lack of toxicity and plenty of physical-chemical properties compared to traditional metal-based quantum dots. CQDs spread effectively in water and are excellent for modifying organic and inorganic materials. Particularly, CQDs show excellent up-conversion photoluminescence characteristics that vary with excitation wavelength and quantum dot size. CQDs' are attractive materials for photocatalysis [21] and can capture and transport electrons, which is necessary for enhancing photocatalytic activity. It was expected that photocatalytic would be enhanced by using MWCNTs.

Dielectrics are gaining academic attention due to their prospective use in tiny current microelectronics and high-power density memory systems. The dielectric properties of CQDs were reported in the literature [22]. The presence of sp2/sp3 hybridization in the CQDs has resulted in a significant increase in permittivity, dielectric strength and conductivity, creating a strong coupling and dipolar ordering in the systems [23]. An extensive investigation was conducted in this manuscript to explore the dielectric characteristics of CQDs by varying percentages of MWCNTs (1,2,3,4,5 w.t %) to record enhancement in dielectric properties.

Researchers are developing antimicrobial compounds to fight bacteria, diseases and fungi in industries such as textiles, water purification, medicines and cosmetics. Wenbo Lu et al., used the hydrothermal method and reported that Carbon quantum dots have established bacteriostatic characteristics and can extend food shelf life. It had a significant inhibitory effect on Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus, and the shelf life of food could be extended by 1–3 days [24]. CQDs possess antibacterial properties owing to photo-activation in the ultraviolet region. However, it was important to study the antibacterial properties in the visible light zone to expand its biomedical applications. So, the use of varying percentages of MWCNTs (1,2,3,4,5 %) in CQDs may enhance its antibacterial properties.

CQDs were prepared by hydrothermal carbonization [25,26], solvothermal carbonization [27], microwave-assisted carbonization [28], arc discharge [29], laser ablation [30], electrochemical exfoliation [31], and microemulsion methods [32,33]. Among them, the microemulsion approach was preferred.

The microemulsion method was used to prepare MWCNTs [34]; however, the novelty of this study was the microemulsion route for the synthesis of CQDs by varying MWCNTs (1,2,3,4,5 w.t %). This route has not been reported earlier using MWCNTs. The microemulsion route was chosen because it was simple, inexpensive, biocompatible and would provide a high yield of carbon. In this research article, toluene was used as an oil, anionic sodium dodecyl sulfate as a surfactant and tween 20 as a non-ionic co-surfactant, keeping all quantities constant. No one has yet used these surfactants and co-surfactants to synthesize CQDs via the microemulsion technique. Additionally, the precursors used were non-toxic and environmentally friendly. A major goal of this research was to synthesize CQDs and evaluate their crystal growth, morphology, dielectric, optical, electrical, photocatalytic, magnetic and antibacterial activity. This was the first study to synthesize CQDs by varying MWCNTs (1–5 w.t %) and study their characteristics. There was no literature available on the anti-bacterial, optical, dielectric and magnetic characteristics of CQDs with varying MWCNT concentrations (1–5 wt. %) via microemulsion technique.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental details of synthesis of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The microemulsion method was employed in this work for the fabrication of Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) by varying percentages of MWCNTs (1–5 wt. %). For the preparation of the microemulsion solution, 40 % of deionized water and 40 % of toluene (oil) were mixed and then magnetically stirred at 1400 rpm for about 30 min until it became bright yellow (clear mixture). Surfactant 10 % Sodium dodecyl sulfate was added to the mixture and stirred well to ensure proper emulsification and provided heating at 70 ⁰C. In the next step, 1–5 wt. % of Multi-walled Carbon Nanotubes (MWCNTs) were mixed into the microemulsion sols and stirred for 1 hour. Then, 10 % Co-surfactant Tween-80 was added into the microemulsion solution and stirred for a further half an hour. During this process, the solution changed its color from bright yellow to black as the MWCNTs were introduced into it. In this way, five samples were prepared separately by changing wt. % of MWCNTs. The solutions were ultrasonicated for further dispersion and uniformity. After cooling down the reaction mixture to room temperature, a few drops of NaOH were added to stop the reaction and stabilize the carbon dots. Afterward, solutions were aged for 48 h at room temperature and were annealed in a muffle furnace at 400 °C for 48 h before characterizations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of working electrode for electrochemical properties
ce:section.ce:para: The active material of 0.08 g (80 wt. %) of CQDs with varying percentages of MWCNTs 1–5 wt. %, 10 wt. % carbon black and polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) each were stirred for 8 h with 2–3 drops of N-Methyle pyrrolidone. It was coated onto a nickel foil (1 × 1 cm2) substrate.

Before deposition, these foils were firstly cleaned with diluted HCL, rinsed with distilled water and dried in an oven set at 70 °C for half an hour. A slurry of active material was continuously stirred, then spread out on a nickel foam and dried in an oven for three hours to serve as the working electrode.

The agar well diffusion method was used to examine the antibacterial activity of CQDs against four different strains of bacteria, and all materials were autoclaved before performing the activity. A stock solution of samples with the lowest and highest MWCNTs i.e. 1 and 5 wt. % dimethyl formamide (DMF) content was prepared until the final amount reached 40 mg/ml. The active dilutions of 2.5 mg/ml, 5 mg/ml, 10 mg/ml, and 20 mg/ml were obtained from the stock solutions made by using DMF. The nutrient agar was first mixed in distilled water and then autoclaved for 20 min at 120 °C to create the culture medium. The medium was cooled and then poured into autoclaved petri plates to solidify at room temperature before being refrigerated. The complete bacteria were sub-cultured on agar plates by transferring them to 4 solidified agar plates using an autoclaved red-hot protecting tip to assess antibacterial activity. After that, the plates were incubated at 37 °C overnight (24 h). Four colonies of a particular bacterial strain were placed into sterile test tubes with 10 ml of saline solution and vortexed thoroughly to make bacterial dilutions. Sterilized cotton buds were utilized to introduce bacteria onto the petri dishes. The uppermost layer of solidified agar plates was wiped with prepared bacterial dilutions such that the bacterium covered the plate surfaces. After that, each petri plate was aligned and placed in a continuous, linear airflow chamber for 15 min. Four equal-distance wells, each measuring 8 mm in diameter, were drilled into the plates using sanitized red hot cork borers. Every plate has an appropriate label. One hundred ml of the material were transferred to the well using a micropipette. To evaluate the bacterial growth, all of the prepared petri dishes were then incubated for 24 h at 37 °C. A clear ruler was then used to measure the inhibitory zone's diameter in millimeters.

Methylene blue (MB) degradation was used to assess the photocatalytic efficacy of varying percentages of MWCNTs (1–5) wt. % in the presence of direct sunlight radiation. Methylene blue is a cationic dye having the chemical formula C₁₆H₁₈N₃SCl. To get a homogenous solution, stir it with a magnetic stirrer for approximately half an hour. The absorption equilibrium of methylene blue in the colloidal solution was then achieved by dissolving 0.0002 g/l of methylene blue in 500 ml of distilled water while stirring constantly for 30 min in the dark. Following that, five prepared dilutions were made from 0.2 ml of M.B. solution, which is a potentially dangerous inorganic component. To ensure that there was no reaction, a control experiment was conducted in the dark using MB to determine the absorption edge. After that, the dilutions were exposed to sunlight for 60 min, a UV–VIS-NIR spectrophotometer was used to measure the variation of the absorption edge at regular intervals of 15 min in order to assess the photo-degradation of MB dye. This experiment was carried out in November.

XRD is an effective technique for identifying crystalline phases of a material and analyze the structural features such as crystallite size, stress, strain and phase composition. XRD spectra were recorded by X-ray powder diffractometer (Advance D8 Bruker) with Cu-kα radiation (λ = 0.154 nm) to determine the influence of varying percentages of MWCNTs (1–5 wt. %) on the structural characteristics of CQDs, as shown in Fig. 1 (a). CQDs have high crystallinity based on the intensity and sharpness of the peaks. From the spectra of (Fig. 1(a)), there were peaks observed at 26.06, 31.32, 33.48, 37.25,45.72, 51.9, 56.5, corresponding to d-spacings of 3.41, 2.85, 2.67, 2.40, 1.98, 1.77, and 1.62 nm, respectively. All peaks exhibited similarity with the XRD pattern for carbon and overall, this pattern agreed well with what was known for turbostratic carbon (24C).

The peak at 26.06⁰ was retained from the MWCNTs, but there was also a new peak at 31.32⁰, and all other peaks were shifted to a slightly smaller angle than seen in JCPDS card No 18–311. The changes may be due to the distortion of the lattices as they were split into smaller units. The new peak at 31.32⁰ may be due to the distortion of the planes or emerged from the small structure generated during the reaction, as similar peaks were recorded in the XRD pattern of CNTs [35]. The peaks at longer angles were the consequence of binding between lattices, leading to the reduction in the d-spacing. There was no indication of any impurity peak and all observed planes verified the development of the cubic phase.

The crystalline size (D) of the samples was evaluated by using the following Scherrer formula [36,37],
D
=
0.9 λ
β cos θ

Where wavelength λ of Cu Kα radiation is 1.54 Å, β is the full width at half maxima (FWHM) and θ is the Bragg angle. The crystallite size was calculated using the highest intensity peak along (002) plane. It was observed that, as the MWCNTs concentration increased, the crystallite size decreased, inhibiting grain development as shown in Table 1 . This decrement in crystallite size might be due to the defects that could be created during the annealing process of CQDs [41]. The surface states of CQDs may alter as a result of annealing, potentially enhancing the production of surface defects. The CQDs may undergo surface atom gain or loss as a result of the thermal energy, which could result in reorganization or even a reduction in crystallite size as defect-driven fragmentation created smaller domains [42]. During the synthesis process, tiny crystallites interacted with one another and developed into larger crystallites. This was one of the most effective strategies to boost the strength of CQDs semiconductor materials.

The dislocation density (δ) in a crystal structure reveals its defects. The presence of dislocations has a substantial effect on the characteristics of the synthesized material. Defects and flaws determine dislocation density, which varies inversely with crystallite size.
δ
=
1
D 2

The three mechanisms that caused defects were (1) the development of grain boundaries, (2) the nucleation process and (3) the establishment of a surface-to-crystal lattice interaction. Smaller crystallite sizes result in larger dislocations in the physical and mechanical characteristics of the respective materials. This resulted in CQDs with the smallest crystallite size, the highest dislocation density and the maximum hardness.

Strain and stress were developed in the material during the synthesis process. The existence of strain in the synthesized CQDs can be calculated by using the following formula,
ε
=
β
4
tan θ

The strain followed a noticeable rising trend in strain values with an increase in MWCNTs content as shown in Table 1. Dislocation density and strain were inversely related to the crystallite size. As the crystallite size decreased, large and broad peaks were identified resulting in the formation of microstrain in the crystal structure caused by twinning and dislocation [38].

The stress induced in CQDs during crystal development from thermal contractions, Phase separation and plastic deformations was calculated using the following equation.
σ

Where ε denotes micro-strain and Y is the Young modulus.
For cubic structure:
In cubic symmetries, all lattice parameters are equivalent and aligned at right angles to each other, as shown by the following relationship: a = b = c and α= β = γ = 90°

The lattice parameter "a" was derived by using an equation.

Where "d" represents the interatomic distance and Miller indices for diffracting planes are represented by (hkl). Table 1 summarized the findings. Different lattice constants resulted in large lattice distortion.

X-ray density was determined using the equation
ρ x

Z M

Where Z is the number of molecules per unit cell, M is the molecular weight,
N A
stands for Avogadro numbers and V is the cell volume. X-ray density rose as MWCNTs (1–5 wt. %) increased (see Table 1). The surface morphology of CQDs determined their qualities. A higher surface-to-volume ratio resulted in a smaller crystallite size.

Specific surface area (SSA) is the surface property of any material that influences reactions, catalytic processes and absorbance on surfaces. It depends on the size of the crystallites. Surface area per unit mass is referred to as SSA [39] and was evaluated by using the relation,
SSA
=
6 *
10
3
ρ x
D

ρ x
represented X-ray density, while D represented crystallite size. According to Table 1, SSA increased as the content of MWCNTs increased from (1–5 wt. %), which was caused by a reduction in crystallite size. Along with the increased SSA, the surface-to-volume ratio rose. This higher SSA would boost overall performance by enhancing energy storage. As a result, equipment such as supercapacitors would perform more efficiently.

The study of functional groups by Attenuated total reflectance-Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (ATR-FTIR) was a more recent and widely used approach that entailed directly depositing the carbon quantum dots onto an ATR crystal, which could provide details about the functional bonds of the sample. It was a non-destructive method that could give results quickly and with low sample requirements. It is used to determine the functional groups between the nanomaterial and the adsorbed molecules. The absorption bands in the FTIR spectra were the indicative of the vibrational frequencies between the bonds of the atoms in the quantum dots. Fig. 1(b), showed The FTIR spectra of the carbon dots obtained by FTIR Model M 2000 Midac USA in the range 500 and 4000 cm−1. The IR spectra exhibited bands between 1106–1405 cm−1 which were caused by the CO (carbonyl group) and C = C stretching of hydrocarbons showing sp2 hybridization and provided evidence that carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were formed [40]. The 1728cm−1 peak corresponded to stretching vibrations of C = O (carbonyl group). The CO2 molecule's presence at the surface was indicated by the band 2037–2176 cm−1. The presence of dip at 2350 cm−1 was due to C––N bond stretching, indicating the presence of the amine functional group. Weak bands at 2900 cm−1 were stretching vibrations of alkane groups, such as C–H stretching. The band was attributed to O–H stretching vibration in the 3200–3600 cm−1 range, which was brought on by chemically adsorbed H2O molecules on the surface of the carbon dot. Due to the OH group forming the hydroxyl radical (OH*), which was necessary for the breakdown of indigo cramine, it served as a key collector of photo-induced charge carriers. Hence, it was significant for enhancing photocatalytic activity. In the final product, the FTIR spectrum demonstrated the existence of carbon and OH group absorption bands as well as the lack of any impurities. Consequently, the results of the FTIR spectrum indicated that CQDs contained the relevant functional groups.

SEM was an important tool for examining the surface morphology of nanostructures. SEM micrographs in Fig. 1(c) displayed the grain size distribution for varying percentages of MWCNTs (1–5 wt. %). It can be seen that the average grain size of the CQDS changed as the wt. % of MWCNTs increased. These micrographs were utilized by applying J image software to determine grain size. SEM revealed a granular, cloud like surface morphology that was not uniform and although being agglomerated, the average grain size for CQDs for (1–5 wt. % MWCNTs) was found to be 20.02, 20.4, 24, 24.5 and 25 nm (Fig. 1(c)). The granular and agglomerated micrographs showed that there were many nano-grains within the grain. The high density of grain boundaries within these grains significantly improved the magnetic characteristics of the nano-crystalline materials. The grain size increased as grain boundaries shrank. This enhanced the electrical properties of CQDs by facilitating the easy flow of electrons from grain to grain. The micrographs also showed that when the MWCNTs content rose, the grains became prominently agglomerated and void-filled, which might be due to increased surface energy. Table 2 showed that grain size increased as MWCNTs content raised and surface area decreased with the increasing MWCNTs percentage. The porosity of CQDs was found to be 14.77 %, 17.201 %, 14.191 %, 12.482 % and 10.201 % for 1–5 wt. % MWCNTs. This decrement in porosity made them applicable for a wide range of applications, especially in energy storage devices.

Optical parameters are important in evaluating how different MWCNT concentrations (1–5 wt %) influence the optical properties of the synthesized CQDs. At room temperature, the absorption spectra of CQDs with varying MWCNT (1–5 w.t %) were recorded between 200 and 800 nm wavelength range, as shown in Fig. 2 (a) by Hitachi 2800 U UV–visible-NIR spectrophotometer. It can be seen that CQDs exhibited absorption peaks at 278 nm which were matched with the reported literature [37], as illustrated in Fig. 2(a). CO band produced nπ *transition and the conjugated CC band produced ππ * transition. This was consistent with the literature [41]. The absorption peak was found to have a small redshift with the addition of a higher content of MWCNTs due to the quantum confinement effect and altered energy band structure [42]. As the crystallite size of MWCNTs-doped CQDs was small, some localized band gap states might have formed, resulting in this redshift. It can be seen that absorbance increased with the increase of MWCNT concentration. This increase in absorption was due to decreased crystallite size (XRD analysis). This increase could be possible due to minor light loss and a large number of defects. In short, the redshift was due to the formation of localized defect states, charge transfer to MWCNTs and modified band structures. The increase in absorbance was attributed to enhanced light trapping, increased defect density and reduction in scattering losses.

The optical parameters of materials are important to construct and use the electronic devices. Optical parameters were listed in Table 3 such as band gap (Eg), optical conductivity (σ), skin depth (χ), extinction coefficient (k), Urbach tail energy (Eu), real and imaginary components of dielectric constants.

The optical band gap (Eg) provides accurate explanations for optical transitions. The band gap energy (Eg), of varying percentages of MWCNTs was determined using Tauc's relation [43].
α h ν
= A
(
h ν − E g
)
n / 2

In this equation, α represents the absorption coefficient, Eg represents the absorption band gap, A represents the constant, hυ represents photon energy, and n can be 1 or 4, where n = 4 represents the indirect band gap transition and n = 1 is the direct band gap transition. In this research, CQDs have direct band gap energy, as shown in Fig. 2(b), which was calculated by plotting a graph between (αhv)2 and hν.

The band gap for varying MWCNTs (1–5 w.t %) decreased from 3.89 to 3.5 eV (Table 3). Due to the quantum confinement effect, the band gap was influenced by the size of C-dots. The current Eg values agreed well with the previously reported results [44–47].

The band gap decreased as MWCNTs concentration increased (1–5 w.t %). The reduction in the band gap was due to the structural disorder and defect states in the crystal lattice. Due to their lower band gap energy, CQDs were shown to be excellent photocatalysts, extending light absorption into the visible range and utilizing solar energy for pollutant degradation. Due to these fascinating properties, CQDs are ideal for photovoltaic conversion devices as they absorb more visible light.

The extinction coefficient is a feature that measures a material's ability to absorb light at certain wavelengths. The extinction coefficient (k) was explored by equation [48].
K = α λ / 4 π

The incident beam's absorption coefficient and wavelength were denoted by α and λ, respectively. Fig. 3 (a) showed the extinction coefficients of varying concentrations of MWCNTs (1–5 w.t %) versus wavelength. The extinction coefficient was enhanced with the increase in MWCNTs contents, which improved light absorption. The surface roughness of CQDs was increased with the increase MWCNTs contents due to agglomerations which were assessed using SEM imaging,

The optical conductivity was given as,
σ = α n c / 4 π

Where α represents the absorption coefficient, n is the refractive index and c is the velocity of light. Fig. 3(b) showed the optical conductivity of varying concentrations of MWCNTs (1–5 w.t %) versus wavelength. The optical conductivity of CQDs increased with MWCNTs contents due to the increase of charge carriers.
Skin depth is the major factor that depends on photon absorption (1/α) [49].

It was defined as:
χ
=
1 / α

Fig. 3(c) illustrated how skin depth varied with wavelength (nm). Skin depth was determined by the material's permeability and conductivity, with higher conductivity resulting in lower skin depth values (as indicated in Table 3).

A crucial optical property Urbach energy measures material disorders and defects. The Urbach energy displays the microstructural defects and lattice disorders in the localized states. The Urbach energy is defined by
α =
α 0
e x p
(
h ν / E u
)

Where hv is the energy of the photon, α₀ is a constant, and Eu is the Urbach energy. Fig. 3(d) showed how lnα varied with photon energy for different concentrations of MWCNTs. Urbach energy measurements were obtained by taking the inverse of the slope of the linear region of the curve.

By plotting ln α to photon energy (hʋ), the Urbach energy was explored by a linear fit of the reverse slope. The Eu values were presented in Table 3. Table 3 showed that the Urbach energy increased with increasing MWCNTs concentration. This confirmed structural defects and disorder in the localized states of the band gap. A small crystallite size originated from the strain and dislocations in the CQDs, which increased the structural disorder. It affected the grain boundary and thus, disturbance was produced in the density of the states. Therefore, the enhanced Eu value indicated a decrement in the crystallite size due to increased structural disorder.
The complex dielectric constant describes the inherent optical properties of CQDs, including their ability to react to electric fields and induce polarization.
Table 3 displayed the real and imaginary components of the dielectric constant for varying MWCNTs (1–5 w.t %).

The dielectric constant is determined by the relation
ε

The dielectric constant has imaginary and real components, represented by εr and εi respectively. The following relationship determines these:

Where k is the extinction coefficient and n is the refractive index. The imaginary dielectric constant exhibited the pattern of extinction coefficient and real components displayed curve as of refractive index. Table 3 showed that the real component εr was greater than the imaginary component εi for all CQDs. The εr reflected the substance's ability to slow down light and store energy and εi represented the absorption of energy by the movement of dipoles.

Modern electronics are advancing at an impressive rate, but this has motivated researchers to create innovative energy storage devices with high power, high energy and long discharge time. Carbon-based supercapacitors (CSs) store electrical energy at the interfacial position between the carbonaceous electrode surface and the adsorbed electrolyte layer. Due to slow mass dispersion and constrained charge accumulation, commercial CSs that use activated carbons have low energy densities in the organic electrolytes. To overcome these issues, carbon quantum dots with varying percentages of MWCNTs 1–5 wt. % were synthesized using a microemulsion technique to increase the capacity of energy storage by exploring electrodes of large capacitance and electrolytes of high potential. The electrochemical response was examined using a Corrtest galvanostat-potentiostat (model number CS350).

In this present research, CQDs were first examined for their electrochemical characteristics using cyclic voltammetry measurements by a 3-electrode glass cell at various scan rates ranging from 5mVs-1 to 100mVs-1 with 1 M KOH as the electrolyte solution. The characterization process included cyclic voltammetry (CV), galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS).

CV was used to study the characteristic curves in response to an increasing potential difference. The electrochemical redox performance of CQDs electrodes was investigated using CV analysis. CV results for electrodes in 1 M KOH electrolyte solution were shown in Fig. 4 (a), with 5 mVs-1 plots. Additionally, for comparison, the CV of bare nickel foam has also been performed and included in Fig. 4(a) [50].

All of the aforementioned electrodes exhibited pseudo-capacitive behavior, as demonstrated by the non-rectangular CV curves with noticeable redox peaks. The concept behind cyclic voltammetry was to change the working electrode's applied voltage while keeping an eye on the current in both forward and backward directions at predetermined scan rates. These redox peaks presented reversible electrochemical redox reactions occurring at the electrode surface or might be due to the presence of a trace amount of carbonyl group present on the surface of activated carbon [51]. The addition of MWCNTs enhanced electrical conductivity and provided more electroactive sites, facilitating electron transfer between the CQDs and electrolyte. This enhanced the redox activity. Moreover, CQDs often have oxygen-containing groups that participated in redox reactions, contributing to the pseudocapacitive behavior [50,52].

The following relation was used to calculate a working electrode's specific capacitance [53]:
C
s p
=
A
2 m k
(
V 2
−
V
1
)
Where “m” was the loaded active mass within the electrode in grams, “k” was the scan rate in mVs-1. “A” was the area of a cyclic curve and “V2-V1” was potential applied.

The following relation was used to get the average power and energy density value for CQDs supercapacitors:
E d

C

Where t was the discharge period (s) and Csp was the specific capacitance.

V was a potential window. CQDs synthesized from 5 wt. % MWCNTs displayed higher energy and power density than CQDs electrodes prepared from 1–4 wt. % MWCNTs.

The rated capacity of the electrode material was considerably influenced by the GCD characteristics. The galvanostatic charge/discharge curves of CQDs with 1–5 wt. % MWCNTs were shown in Fig. 4(b). The charge and discharge investigations were run in the potential range of −0.1 V to 0.9 V for all electrode materials. The GCD curves showed non-triangular, non-symmetric-shaped behavior for charge-discharge characteristics of the material. Through the use of the discharge curves, the values of specific capacitance were calculated at current densities as shown in Fig. 4(b). A charge separation occurred at the electrode/electrolyte interface and the sample's discharge curves showed the capacitive behavior of the electrode [54]. The capacitive performance was outstanding because the electrode material has high electrical conductivity and a wide specific surface area. The Csp calculation from the GCD and CV curves were not the same. Due to pseudocapacitive behavior, the CV method captured additional charge storage from redox reactions, leading to higher Csp than GCD [55]. To enhance the quantity of redox sites and provide a high electrical conductivity for rapid and effective electron transport and ion charge transfer, MWCNTs were one of the main carbon-based materials used. Further, there were more surface-active sites available for electrolyte exposure, which greatly raised the capacitance. Carbon-based electrode material synthesized from CQDs by varying 1–5 wt. % MWCNNTs was appropriate for supercapacitor applications (Table 4 ).

Keeping in mind the highest energy and power density of CQD electrodes, its coulombic efficiency was examined. A popular method for assessing a supercapacitor's performance is to measure its coulombic efficiency by the relation [56]
η
t
=
t D
t C
* 100
where the times for charging (tC) and discharging (tD) are expressed (s).

The gradual increase in potential during charging (−0.1 V to 0.9 V) reflected the accumulation of charge within the MWCNT composite. The slope of the charging curve was related to the capacitance of the material. A steeper slope indicated a higher capacitance. The fact that it started below 0 V and ended at 0.9 V just meant the material was capable of storing charge within this range. Similarly, the decrease in potential during discharge (0.9 V to 0.6 V) represented the release of stored charge. The slope of the discharge curve also reflected the capacitance. The voltage did not drop to 0 V because there was still some stored charge at 0.6 V and the device's operation was likely limited to this range.
The coulombic efficiency of varying 1–5 wt. % MWCNTs, as displayed in Fig. 4(c), was 78 %, 78.5, 79, 80 and 81.5 %.

The electrochemical method known as EIS was used to investigate the ion transport, intrinsic resistance and interaction of the electrode [57]. CQDs with varying percentages of MWCNTs electrodes have all undergone EIS studies at 5 mV in the frequency range 10−1 - 105 Hz. Nyquist plot was used to analyze the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) data between impedance imaginary part (Z″) and real part (Z′). To fit the EIS graphs and equivalent circuit, Z-View software was used (Fig. 4(d) inset). All five electrodes displayed the standard AC impedance properties of supercapacitors [58]. The following two results were drawn in the high-frequency range (Fig. 4(d) inset):
1)
The radius of the semicircle presented the charge-transfer resistance (Rct) of the electrode and the conductive ability of the electrode material.
2)
The real axis intercept indicated a combined resistance Rs comprising the ionic resistance of the solution of electrolyte, the resistance of the electrodes and the interaction resistance between the current collector and electrode.

Since the resistance of the electrolyte solution remained unchanged, the lower Rs of 5 wt. % MWCNTs (0.0901 Ω) in comparison to 1–4 wt. % MWCNTs (0.9401 Ω), (0.9651 Ω), (0.980Ω), and (0.970 Ω) revealed decreased electrode resistance, which was in agreement with the CV and GCD results [59]. Simultaneous with protonic ion exchange, electrolyte ion agglomeration at the electrode-electrolyte interface resulted in a semicircle zone on the Nyquist graphs at high frequency [60]. The charge transfer resistance (Rct) computed using the diameter of the semicircle was the outcome of those occurrences [60].

5 wt. % MWCNTs (1.80821 Ω) had a significantly lower Rct as Compared to 1 wt. % MWCNTs (2.9092 Ω), 2 wt. % MWCNTs (2.8081 Ω), 3 wt. % MWCNTs (2.7665 Ω), 4 wt. % MWCNTs (2.7560 Ω) per the reported literature [61]. This suggested that the CQDs synthesized from varying percentages of MWCNTs (1–5 wt. %) had a high surface area which developed a better electrolyte-active material connection and less resistance during charge transfer (Fig. 4(d) insert).

In the intermediate frequency range, the Warbrug resistance symbolized the ion diffusion and transport in the electrolyte as shown by the slope of the 45° section of the curves. For CQDs with 5 wt. % MWCNTs, the electrode's capacitive behavior was observed on the curve in the low-frequency range. Despite a minor bend in the curvature, the capacitive performance was generally good. It can be seen that increasing MWCNTs content led to the decrement in Rs and Rct values due to the highly conductive nature of 5 wt. % MWCNTs. The findings of the EIS were in good agreement with those of other electrochemical methods. These experimental findings suggested that 5 wt. % CQDs was a perfect material for supercapacitor electrodes.

The dielectric properties were measured with an Agilent E4980A Precise LCR meter. The insertion of materials into various optoelectronic gadgets such as light-emitting diodes, laser technology, photovoltaic devices and optical fibers requires the study of the dielectric properties [66].
Impedance spectroscopy was used to measure the capacitance and dissipation factors of CQDs at room temperature. The frequency range for the experiments was set between 20 Hz to 20 MHz.

The dielectric constant was calculated using the following equation:
ε

Where ε0 represented the permittivity of free space whose value is (8.85* 10–12 Fm-1), A is the cross-sectional area, Cp is the capacitance of pallet and thickness by d. Every parameter was measured using SI units. This equation gives the real portion of the dielectric constant (
ε

), which represents the degree of polarization. The dielectric constant was predominantly high at high frequencies, as shown in Fig. 5 (a).

Due to intrinsic energy loss, signal loss is measured by the imaginary dielectric constant, often known as the loss factor or tangent loss. The amount of energy wasted in a material under an alternating electric field is measured by the imaginary dielectric constant, often known as the loss factor. It displays the dielectric loss and energy absorption of the material. To calculate the tangent loss, the following equation was used
t a n
δ =
1
2 π f R C

Where,
t a n
δ
is the tangent loss angle, f denotes the frequency of the applied alternating electric field, R is the resistance and C denotes the material capacitance. As frequency increased, dielectric loss decreased. Large dielectric loss was caused by defect accumulation in CQDs at the low-frequency region, which can be described by the Maxwell-Wagner (M-W) phenomenon. In nanoscale systems, electrons can tunnel across grain boundaries via field-assisted tunneling or direct tunneling, reducing resistance. It suggested that charge carriers were unable to travel between grains because of the resistive interfaces [67]. Thus, only a small number of charge carriers can tunnel at low frequencies, but at higher frequencies, the charge carriers have sufficient energy to overcome the obstacle and can tunnel across the grains. Table 5 and Fig. 5(b). illustrated a decreasing trend as the percentage of MWCNTs rose. Due to the decreased dielectric tan loss, this material was perfect for a wide range of high-frequency purposes, such as supercapacitors.

A material's conductivity is greatly influenced by several factors such as Interstitial spaces, voids, secondary phases and free charge carriers. Electrical conduction in a material is caused by the movement of electrons between grain boundaries leading to higher conductivity. The AC conductivity of CQDs can be calculated by using the equation:

Fig. 5(c) showed that with the increase in frequency, AC conductivity was also increased as the concentration of MWCNTs increased (1–5 wt. %). This happened because the electron hopping process increased the drift mobility of holes as well as electrons. Additionally, at low frequencies, charge carriers were trapped in localized states and contributed less to conduction and at higher frequencies, the alternating field provided sufficient energy to enable hopping transitions, which increased the conductivity.

Table 5 demonstrated that the Rmin increased as a result of an increase in MWCNTs contents. The Austine Mott relation indicated that electron jumping in the confined N(Ef) states at the Fermi level was necessary for ac conductivity. where e, fo and α represented the electronic charge, frequency of photon and the localized wave function. To calculate the density of localized states N(Ef), α and fo was set to 1010 m-1 and 1013 Hz. The plot of N (EF) against frequency was shown in Fig. 5(d). It showed that the density of the state increased as the wt. % of MWCNTs increased. Charge carrier hopping was expected to replace charge transfer, according to the large N (EF).

A Lake Shore 7407 vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM) is a scientific equipment used to measure the magnetic properties of materials. A changing magnetic field in VSM causes a corresponding change in the electric field. Generally, pure carbon nanotubes showed diamagnetism. It means that ferromagnetic behavior was not a built-in property of the carbon nanotubes. The CQDs were not ferromagnetic but in this work, CQDs were synthesized by a simple solution-based microemulsion route, which improved their ferromagnetic properties. As synthesized CQDs were annealed at 400 °C which stimulated partial graphitization with enhancement in the sp²/sp³ ratio of CQDs. The boosted π-electron delocalization producing exchange interactions between spin-polarized states, leading to enhanced ferromagnetism. The carbon quantum dots have low magnetic properties at low concentrations of MWCNTs. In contrast, at high concentrations of MWCNTs, the long-range exchange interaction and formation of bound polarons through carbon p orbitals generated ferromagnetism. This was due to oxygen vacancies created at the surface of the CQDs. Such vacancies led to nonzero net spin. The different valence states resulted in saturation magnetization which increased with decreasing crystallite size. The system shows a multiferroic behavior below a critical size of the nanoparticles and the related polarization tends to a saturation value when the particle size is enhanced. The smaller particles produced a larger saturation magnetization showing an increase in surface defects with the decrease in particle size. This observed ferromagnetism can be linked with the oxygen vacancies at the surface of CQDs.

The microemulsion technique produced defect sites and oxygen vacancies within the lattice. The presence of bonded magnetic polarons at these defect locations led to a small improvement in ferromagnetic behavior, as seen in Fig. 6 . After the synthesis of CQDs, an annealing process was also applied which could be favorable to optimize their ferromagnetic behavior. Annealing can influence material properties by reducing defects, modifying structural characteristics, and optimizing magnetic properties, potentially making the CQDs more effective for their intended application. The physical factors of CQDs, such as porosity, chemical composition, grain size and density significantly influenced the area and shape of the hysteresis curve. The hysteresis curves were plotted between the change of magnetic moment Vs magnetic field for varying percentages of MWCNTs (1–5 wt. %) as shown in Fig. 6.

The value of coercive force lied between 1045–1416 Oe while saturation magnetization was in the range 11.06–14.090emu-cm-3. Table 6 illustrated coercivity, remanence magnetization, saturation magnetization and squareness ratios for hysteresis loops with MWCNTs (1–5) wt. %. These values indicated that as the percentage of MWCNTs increased, the carbon quantum dots showed a little ferromagnetic behaviour, making them suitable for spintronics technology.

In addition, the magnetic characteristics were highly dependent on the grain size. Grain size was increased by increasing MWCNTs concentration as reported in SEM, which led to a decrease in surface-to-volume ratio due to the presence of a significant number of grain boundaries. The rotation of magnetic domains produced magnetization and demagnetization in a substance. As the number of grain boundaries increased, the domain needed more energy for magnetization and demagnetization so they had a large coercivity. Crystalline size and coercivity have an inverse relation [69]. Crystallite size was decreased as the content of MWCNTs increased which was in good agreement with the increased value of coercivity (see Table 6).

Lorentz's microscopic investigations suggested that grain boundaries acted as pinning locations for the magnetic domain wall's movement [70]. In the present research, it was noted that the pinning effect of magnetic domain walls increased due to the grain boundaries which was the reason for the increment in the values of saturation magnetization (Ms) and remanence magnetization Mr as shown in Table 6.
The "squareness ratio rose as the contents of MWCNTs increased. CQDs having Mr/Ms value > 1 were optimized for use in data storage devices [71].

A squareness ratio of all samples possessed a very low remanent magnetization than the saturation magnetization. It means that when the external magnetic field was removed, the material lost its magnetization. These materials were not ideal for magnetic storage devices.

Photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy is a successful technique to explore the optical characteristics of material. An electron absorbs a photon whose energy lies in the visible region, excites it to a higher excited energy state, and then emits a photon when the electron returns to a lower energy state (ground state) due to recombination with the hole. PL spectra have been frequently utilized to indicate the charge movement, migration and possibility of electron-hole pair recombination phenomena in the catalyst CQDs. Decreased intensity indicated a reduced recombination possibility of photo-excited charge carriers [72], since the PL emission spectra were obtained from this process. Fig. 7 presented the PL spectra for CQDs synthesized by varying (1–5 wt. %) MWCNTs at room temperature in the wavelength range 320–560 nm. The emission band was observed between 410 and 450 nm in the visible region, which was often associated with surface defects and internal point defects. Although 1, 2 wt. % MWCNTs had the highest intensity it represented a low chance of electron and hole separation, but it dropped gradually as the percentage of MWCNTs increased from 3–5 wt. % as seen in PL spectra Fig. 7. The reduction in emission band intensity might be due to the agglomeration and formation of large particles, which was a good agreement with SEM. Based on the aforementioned experimental results, it was expected that the insertion of 5 wt. % MWCNTs reduced the rate of electron and hole recombination, allowing for more efficient charge separation of CQDS and thus making it more suitable for optoelectronic applications and photo-catalysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic degradation study of methylene blue (MB) solution under visible light
ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, Environmental contamination, particularly water pollution, is an alarming worldwide issue. Organic dyes and their waste products are a major cause of water pollution. The wastewater treatment plants in mining industries, fabric industry, paper printing, food production, cosmetics, pharmaceuticals and tanning are need of the day.

Dyes are a major cause of water contamination, with 1–15 % discharged as contaminants during the dyeing procedure. Textile dye including methylene blue (C16H18N3SCl) can affect the circulatory system, skin and genes by creating hazardous additives [72]. Removing these contaminants from wastewater is a major issue. Therefore, it is important to find efficient methods to remove dyes from wastewater. Activated carbon removed dye via van der Waals forces, electrostatic interactions, and π-π stacking. To remove and decompose harmful contaminants, biological techniques including aerobic, adsorption, sedimentation and advanced oxidation processes are used. Among these techniques, advanced oxidation via photo-catalytic degradation is chosen over other techniques. This approach is cost-effective, environmentally harmless, and completely removes colours without producing any additional contaminants. Photo-catalytic processes rely heavily on electronic band structure and energy. A photo-catalyst with a large surface area, low cost and no toxic byproducts can improve photo-catalytic reaction rates [73].

Using methylene blue as an organic pollutant, the photocatalytic activity of photocatalysts CQDs synthesized by varying (1–5 wt. %) MWCNTs was evaluated in this study. Table 7 (a) and the Fig. 8 (a), represented the absorption spectra of CQDs with (1–5 wt. %) MWCNTs and without the methylene blue.

Photocatalyst CQDs degraded MB when exposed to sunlight, indicated by the shifting of the absorption edge toward the visible region of the spectrum. Table 7(b) displayed the shifting of the absorption edge for CQDS with 1–5 wt. % of MWCNTs after equal intervals of time 0, 15, 30, 45 and 60 min having 0.2 ml of MB solution in the presence of sunlight. The absorption edge shifted to the visible region of the spectrum when exposed to sunlight, suggesting MB deterioration, as shown in Table 7(b). After 60 min of sunshine exposure, Fig. 8(b) displayed the shifted spectra of CQDs. The study suggested that by increasing the percentage of MWCNTs, the photocatalytic properties of CQDs were enhanced.

The MB breakdown was identified by analyzing the reduction in its absorption peak at 662 nm (see Fig. 8(c)). Fig. 8(c) demonstrated that CQDs reduced the intensity of the absorption peak for 5 wt. % MWCNTs content with an increase in the interval of sunlight exposure. Photocatalytic efficiency was determined by the ability of CQDs to absorb light and produce separation of photon-induced charge carriers. 5 wt. % MWCNTs demonstrated the strongest photocatalytic activity due to their low band gap energy, allowing valence electrons to be driven by moderately intense photons. The high percentage of MWCNTs-induced defects enhanced degradation by limiting electron-hole recombination.

The photocatalytic electron-hole recombination mechanism was shown in Fig. 8(d). The mechanism depicted that photocatalytic activity requires both sources of light and catalyst. CQDs acted as a photo catalyst breaking down organic pollutants like MB when exposed to sunlight. When sunlight hit the CQDs, it absorbed light and transferred a photo-excited electron from valence to the conduction band, creating an electron-hole pair (Fig. 8(d)). It may be concluded from the experimental data that carbon quantum dots were crucial for increased photocatalytic activity. The separation of electrons and holes was the suggested mechanism [74].

As a result of these reactive oxygen species (ROS) such as •O2 −and O
H ·
radicals, the MB molecule degraded. Photocatalytic electrolysis is an innovative and adaptable technique that uses sunlight to induce chemical processes, with applications including water purification and environmental safety. The photocatalytic activity also showed that CQDs had antibacterial properties due to its redox activity and byproducts including ROS (reactive oxygen species) and hydroxyl ions, which can fight against viruses and microbes to fight against viruses and microbes.

The degrading efficiency of CQDs was calculated using the following equation [75]
η

where Co represents the starting amount and C shows the content at time t.

Table 8 (a) showed that degradation efficiency was greatly improved with the increasing percentage of MWCNTs (1–5 wt. %). As MWCNTs have a huge surface area, this results in more active sites for photocatalytic processes. The large surface area enhanced the interaction between carbon dots and target contaminants, resulting in improved degradation. The large surface area also introduced surface defects such as oxygen vacancies, structural distortions and functional groups. These defects served as electron traps, prolonging the charge carrier lifetime and enhancing the degradation efficiency.

Fig. 9(a) showed the normalized cyclic fluctuations in MB content
(
c
c 0
)
throughout the photocatalytic activity for CQDS with (1–5 wt. % MWCNTs). The photocatalytic activity of CQDs in sunshine at 1 wt. % MWCNTs was low 9.09 % (∼9.09 %). The photocatalyst (CQDs) had the highest degrading efficiency of (∼11.11 %) after 1 hour of irradiation of CQDs with 5 wt. % MWCNTs. Finally, Oxidative reactive species, especially hydroxyl radicals and other reactive species, decolorized methylene blue by oxidizing its molecular structure.

Fig. 9 (b) depicted the kinetics analysis. The ln(C/Co) Vs time graph displayed the kind and nature of the reaction types. For Pseudo-First Order, the rate equation was written as follows [76]:
ln

− k t

The above equation represented a straight line. The degradation rate constant (k min-1) can be calculated by using the slope of the straight line between
l n (

)
Vs time. Fig. 9(b) and Table 9(a) demonstrated that chemical processes were First-Order in nature. The plot was linear and First-Order had the highest value 0.017 min-1. The study demonstrated that CQDs effectively removed the MB dye.

The real-world use of photo catalysts was heavily dependent on their reusability. To evaluate the long-term stability of CQDs catalysts in the photocatalytic breakdown of MB, the catalysts were separated, washed with distilled water and collected for further cycles.

Fig. 9(c), demonstrated the possibility of reuse of CQDs with various MWCNTs contents (1–5 wt. %) after three cycles of MB decay and exposure to sunshine for an hour. Table 9(b) illustrated the recycling efficiency calculated using degrading efficiency and (k min-1) for three cycles of CQDs with 1–5 wt. % of MWCNTs. The photocatalytic activity almost reduced after three cycles, indicating the catalyst's recyclability. Sustainable recycling of catalysts after photocatalytic reactions might be suitable for commercial applications.

The antibacterial activity of CQDs synthesized by varying 1 −5 wt . % of MWCNTs was investigated against four distinct bacterial strains: Klebsiella pneumoniae (K.P), Staphylococcus aureus (S.A), E. coli (E.C) and Salmonella enterica (S.E).

Of these four bacterial strains, only S. aureus was gram-positive; the others were all gram-negative. This was a first step because there was no literature on the antibacterial activity of CQDs synthesized from MWCNTs. The pathogens were cultivated on a nutrient agar plate at 37 °C. Materials diffused more quickly in solid medium and prevented the growth of both gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria by forming inhibitory zones, according to a qualitative study of the antibacterial activity of stock solutions.

Outcomes of antibacterial properties against the Staphylococcus aureus (S.A), Salmonella enterica (S.E), Klebsiella pneumoniae (K.P) and E. coli (E.C) bacteria were shown in Fig. 10 (a). The CQDs synthesized from 5 wt. % MWCNTs showed significant antibacterial action as compared to the 1 wt. % MWCNTs at a high dilution 20 mg/ml. A small dissolving ratio resulted in a small inhibitory zone (Fig. 10(b) bar graphs).

By mixing a higher dilution ratio, a larger zone of inhibition was generated which also actually increased the penetration ability of nanoparticles into the agar media and the elimination of bacteria. According to the findings, the 5 wt. % samples' zones of inhibition against K. pneumoniae, S. aureus, E. coli and S. enterica were 25± 0.70 mm, 22 ± 1.41 mm, 25± 0.7 and 28 ± 2.12 mm, respectively (Fig. 10(a) and Table 10 ). It was noted that the antibacterial properties of the CQDs were significantly enhanced by the addition of a high percentage of MWCNTs, as evidenced by the 5 wt. % MWCNTs with an inhibition zone of 28 ± 2.12 mm against Salmonella Enterica (Table 10). This demonstrated that MWCNTs's highest content could be beneficial in other antibacterial applications. Many scientists have proposed suggestions regarding the mechanisms of interaction between biological compounds and nanoparticles. Microbes were known to have a negative charge, while CQDs were sometimes known to have a positive charge. After that, there was an electromagnetic attraction between the microbe and the treated surface. After coming into contact with the blood, the bacterium immediately died due to lack of oxygen. Hydrogen peroxide, which CQDs generated, destroyed biomolecules and encouraged the formation of hydroxyl radicals, possibly leading to cell death. The bacterial enzyme catalase may oxidize hydrogen peroxide to create oxygen and water [72]. Therefore, to make sure that the H2O2 level surpassed the elimination threshold and caused damage to cells, high MWCNTs content was required. So, it was concluded that CQDs synthesized from MWCNTs created a powerful antibacterial system that disrupted the cell walls and metabolic processes of bacteria, especially S. enterica. The purpose of this study was to explore how the concentration of MWCNTs affected antibacterial activity. Effective antibacterial action has been observed for the CQDs synthesized from 5 wt. % when combined with membrane dissection, oxidative stress and intracellular interference. The nanoparticles damaged the cell membranes and ultimately caused the cell to die when they adhered to the cell walls of bacteria. Additionally, it entered cells and stopped the creation of proteins, messenger RNA (mRNA) and enzymes. The nanoparticles' thinner peptidoglycan coating and outer membrane made it more efficient against Gram-positive bacteria. In general, the results showed that CQDs, especially those with a high MWCNTs content, have beneficial prospects for use in biological sectors such as the reconstruction of tissues, medication administration and healing of wounds. These quantum dots may be effective against Diarrhea, vomiting, fever, and abdominal cramps as they have shown good antibacterial activity against salmonella enterica.

The microemulsion method was used to synthesize carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with the variation of 1- 5 wt. % of multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs). Structural analysis confirmed the formation of the cubic phase of carbon and decrement in crystallite size with the increase in MWCNTs contents. The band gap was reduced by adding MWCNTs. CQDs can be used in solar cells as the decrease in band gap allowed solar cells to absorb more visible light, increasing their efficiency. Their narrow band gap made them ideal for water treatment and absorption layers in photovoltaic cell technology. At varying MWCNTs concentration (1–5 wt. %), the cubic phase of CQDs showed strong ionic stability, resulting in an enhanced specific capacitance range of (1007.81–4531.25 Fg-1), power density (174,150–783,000 WKg-1) and energy density (1161–5220 WhKg-1). The tan loss decreased, but the dielectric constant and AC conductivity increased with MWCNTs. Due to the high dielectric constant, these CQDs can be used in energy storage device applications. At room temperature, CQDs with MWCNTs 5 wt. % exhibited the highest saturation magnetization, demonstrating ferromagnetic ordering and making them ideal for spintronics. CQDs might be a potential material for biomedical applications as an antimicrobial and disinfectant. These CQDs have been widely accepted for potential use in supercapacitors, water treatment and medicine.

This work is original, has never been published, and isn't being viewed for publication elsewhere at the moment. For the editorial process, only the Corresponding Author may be contacted. The corresponding author is in charge of keeping the other writers informed about progress, revision submissions, and final proofreading approval. The work has been viewed and authorized by all of the stated authors. All of the writers have agreed on the manuscript's authorship order.

Ayesha Akram: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Zohra Nazir Kayani: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Project administration, Conceptualization.
